questions.tip: Panorama is Palo Alto's NMS. It centralizes: Templates (network/device config, interfaces, routing, zones), Device Groups (objects + policies reused across firewalls), Log Collection (with dedicated Log Collectors), pre-built and custom Reports, and software/panorama updates.

explanations.A: Incorrect: Panorama orchestrates, does not replace firewalls.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the complete role.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it does much more than reports.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is centralized, not just downloading.

questions.question: What is the difference between a 'Template' and a 'Device Group' in Panorama?

options.text: They are synonyms.

Template contains platform configuration (interfaces, routing, zones, IKE gateways, services). Device Group contains objects + reusable policies (address objects, service objects, security policies, NAT, profiles).
Template is for policies; Device Group is for networks.
Device Group is only for logs.

questions.tip: Template = network/device config (e.g., 'interface ethernet1/1 is Layer3, zone Trust, IP 10.1.1.1/24'); Device Group = shared policies and objects. The separation allows changing the network without touching policies (and vice versa). Firewalls can inherit from multiple templates and DGs.

explanations.A: Incorrect: they have distinct roles.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the conceptual separation.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is the opposite.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Device Group is for objects and policies, not for logs.

questions.question: In Panorama, what is 'commit all' vs 'commit push' selective?

options.text: They are synonyms.
'Commit All' commits EVERYTHING to ALL managed firewalls. 'Commit push' selective commits only partial changes to selected firewalls, ideal for gradual changes.
'Commit All' is only for templates.
'Push' selective is for logs.

questions.tip: 'Commit All' vs 'Push' selective: for critical changes (e.g., software update), 'Commit All' to all firewalls at once. For gradual changes (e.g., new policy you want to test on a subset), 'Push' selective allows for progressive rollout. ALWAYS use 'Validate Commit' beforehand to detect errors.

explanations.A: Incorrect: they have different purposes.
explanations.B: Correct: distinguishes the deployment modes.
explanations.C: Incorrect: 'Commit All' applies everything.
explanations.D: Incorrect: push is for config, not logs.

questions.question: Your Panorama manages 30 firewalls. How many Log Collectors should you have?

options.text: Only one.

Multiple (Dedicated Log Collectors), one or more, sized according to the total log volume and required retention. It is recommended to separate the Log Collector from the Management Server for performance.
Zero; Panorama does not use log collectors.
As many as firewalls.

questions.tip: Dedicated Log Collectors (even on dedicated or virtual hardware) improve performance and scalability. The number depends on the total EPS (Events Per Second) and the required retention (days/months/years). A good practice: estimate EPS = N firewalls * EPS per firewall, size accordingly.

explanations.A: Incorrect: only one does not scale.
explanations.B: Correct: describes good sizing practice.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is an option, not mandatory but recommended.
explanations.D: Incorrect: 1:1 is not necessary.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a 'device group hierarchy' (parent/child) in Panorama?

options.text: To organize firewalls hierarchically by geography/function (e.g., Parent 'Global', Child 'US-East', 'US-West'; or Parent 'Branches', Child 'Branch-Madrid'). Allows selective override of inherited rules.
It is decorative.
To group licenses.
Only for reporting.

questions.tip: The Device Groups hierarchy allows inheriting policies/objects and applying specific overrides where they differ (e.g., parent rule 'allow HTTPS inbound' but child adds regional restriction). It is key for organizations with many sub-structures: HQ -> regions -> branches.

explanations.A: Correct: explains the hierarchical organization and overrides.
explanations.B: Incorrect: it has real functional value.
explanations.C: Incorrect: licenses are assigned separately.
explanations.D: Incorrect: reporting is orthogonal.

questions.question: A push of changes to a firewall fails with 'dependency conflict'. How to resolve?

options.text: Reinstall the firewall.
Use 'Revert' to the previous commit; investigate the conflict (referenced object deleted, duplicate IP address, etc.); fix in Panorama and push again.
Ignore the error.
Only affects this firewall.

questions.tip: Dependency conflict means the firewall has local config that conflicts with the config Panorama is trying to push. Example: rule 'A' in Panorama references object 'X' that the firewall locally no longer has (because it was deleted locally without commit). Resolution: 'Revert' the firewall to the previous state, or rewrite the local config to match.

explanations.A: Incorrect: reinstall is invasive.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the remediation flow.
explanations.C: Incorrect: the firewall remains in an inconsistent state.
explanations.D: Incorrect: although it affects one, it requires remediation.

questions.question: What does the 'Local Panorama Managed' vs 'Panorama Managed' option do?

options.text: It is just configuration.
It allows editing policies locally on the firewall (Local), or configuring all policies from Panorama and blocking local changes (Panorama Managed). It is an operational/governance decision.
It is for VPN.
It is for routing.

questions.tip: Local Panorama Managed (formerly 'Panorama mode'): allows local changes. Panorama Managed (formerly 'Local mode' in some versions): blocks direct changes on the firewall - everything must come from Panorama. Useful for organizations that want total centralized control.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it has real operational impact.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the governance difference.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not for VPN.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is not for routing.

questions.question: Why use 'Panorama Plugins' (e.g., Cisco, AWS, Azure) before configuring Device Groups?

options.text: They are optional.

Panorama plugins integrate with cloud services and third-party tools: AWS plugin extracts VPC/subnets/IAM to auto-create address objects and rules; Cisco plugin integrates with ISE for User-ID. They simplify bootstrapping.
They are only used for reporting.
They are for VPN.

questions.tip: Panorama plugins (AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, VMware NSX, Cisco ACI, etc.) automate the import of metadata from those platforms: VPCs, security groups, tags, instances. This allows building policies dynamically with live references to the cloud infrastructure.

explanations.A: Incorrect: they add significant value.
explanations.B: Correct: lists the benefits of integration.
explanations.C: Incorrect: they are for metadata integration.
explanations.D: Incorrect: not specific to VPN.

questions.question: When pushing to a firewall HA pair, what special consideration is there?

options.text: The push is done to each firewall separately.

Panorama can push to the HA pair as a single unit or as two separate units; if one firewall is down, the push can go to the other and synchronize when it comes back. There is also a push order (primary first).
It is incompatible.
It can only be done via CLI.

questions.tip: Panorama treats an HA pair as a logical device for push purposes. The typical order is primary -> secondary. If one is down, Panorama pushes only to the one that is alive (when it comes back, it will synchronize via the HA link). Always use 'Validate Commit' before pushing in HA to avoid inconsistencies.

explanations.A: Incorrect: Panorama automates.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the push strategy in HA.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is fully compatible.
explanations.D: Incorrect: GUI/CLI both support.

questions.question: Panorama supports 'Panorama Plugins' and 'Service Updates' for external integrations. What is the difference between the two?

options.text: They are synonyms.
Service Updates: content (apps, threats, URL categories, geo). Plugins: integrations with third-party (Cisco ISE for User-ID, AWS/Azure for cloud metadata, ServiceNow for ticketing).
Plugins are for licenses.
Service Updates are for hardware.

questions.tip: Service Updates is the content channel: updates the App-ID, Threat Prevention, URL Filtering, GlobalProtect data, etc. signature base. Plugins are integration modules that extend Panorama to communicate with external systems (ISE, AWS, Azure, GCP, NSX, Kubernetes). Complementary: content + integration.

explanations.A: Incorrect: they serve different roles.
explanations.B: Correct: clearly distinguishes.
explanations.C: Incorrect: plugins are for integration.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Service Updates is for content.

questions.question: What is the difference between HA Active/Passive and Active/Active in Palo Alto?

options.text: They are the same.
Active/Passive: only one firewall active, the other synchronized (config + sessions if HA2). Active/Active: both process traffic, each with a subset of active interfaces, sharing the load.
A/A is not supported.
A/P does not allow failover.

questions.tip: A/P is the simplest pattern: one firewall active, the other passive (synchronized but inactive). Failover = role change. A/A distributes interfaces and/or IPs between the two, both process traffic. A/A requires planning of which interfaces/IPs live where.

explanations.A: Incorrect: different architectures.
explanations.B: Correct: describes both modes.
explanations.C: Incorrect: A/A is supported.
explanations.D: Incorrect: A/P failover is its main function.

questions.question: What are HA1 and HA2 in Palo Alto?

options.text: They are the same, aliases.

HA1 = heartbeat and config sync (between management interfaces, control plane). HA2 = session, ARP, routing, IKE/IPSec SA synchronization (between data interfaces, data plane). HA3 optional to carry data traffic when both are active.
HA2 is for logs.
HA1 is for VPN.

questions.tip: HA1 and HA2 have complementary roles: HA1 (control plane) synchronizes config and keeps the pair alive; HA2 (data plane) synchronizes session for uninterrupted failover. Without functional HA2, existing connections are cut during failover (new firewall does not know the session IDs).

explanations.A: Incorrect: they have different roles.
explanations.B: Correct: describes both.
explanations.C: Incorrect: HA2 is for session.
explanations.D: Incorrect: HA1 includes general config, not VPN-specific.

questions.question: I configure HA Active/Passive. The passive firewall is in a 'suspended' (red) state instead of 'passive' (green). What is the likely cause?

options.text: It is physically broken.

The passive is suspended because it cannot synchronize config or state: check HA1/HA2 links, PAN-OS version compatibility, or that the active did not send the config correctly. Or preempt is on and it is in hold-down.
It is normal A/P behavior.
It can only be suspended with a manual command.

questions.tip: The 'suspended' state means the peer is in the HA pair but cannot become active. Common reasons: (1) different incompatible PAN-OS versions, (2) HA1/HA2 link down or misconfigured, (3) received config is corrupted, (4) license mismatch. Use 'show ha state' and 'show ha peer' to diagnose.

explanations.A: Incorrect: If hardware is ok, it would show differently.
explanations.B: Correct: Lists the common causes.
explanations.C: Incorrect: 'suspended' is NOT normal.
explanations.D: Incorrect: It is not typically a manual decision.

questions.question: What does the 'Path Monitor' do in the HA configuration?

options.text: Checks if a specific destination (e.g., upstream gateway or a critical server) is reachable through the firewall. If it fails, it triggers failover.
It is just a dashboard.
It is for logging.
It is for viruses.

questions.tip: Path Monitor is a 'reachability' monitor: ping or HTTP-GET to a destination behind the firewall. If the destination stops responding (after the configured failures), the firewall considers it has lost the path to the upstream and may trigger failover. Useful for detecting ISP failures that the HA1/HA2 link does not detect.

explanations.A: Correct: Explains the role.
explanations.B: Incorrect: It has an operational effect.
explanations.C: Incorrect: It is not for logging.
explanations.D: Incorrect: It is not antivirus.

questions.question: What is 'HA3' and when is it needed?

options.text: It is an alias of HA2.

HA3 is an OPTIONAL link to forward packets between peers when the active firewall fails and the passive assumes the role but the original flows arrived at the wrong peer (active/active with cross interfaces).
It is for logs.
It is for DNS.

questions.tip: HA3 (data link for forward packets) is the cable that the new active uses to deliver to hosts on interfaces that physically live on the peer. In Active/Active with cross-over interfaces, when peer-1 fails, peer-2 cannot deliver packets to its physical interfaces - HA3 transports them to peer-2 via the other peer.

explanations.A: Incorrect: It is different from HA2.
explanations.B: Correct: Explains the role.
explanations.C: Incorrect: It is not for logs.
explanations.D: Incorrect: It is not DNS.

questions.question: What does 'preempt' mean in HA configuration?

options.text: It is an attack.

If enabled, after a failover, the primary firewall resumes the active role when it comes back (instead of remaining passive by default). If disabled, the secondary stays active (recommended to avoid flapping).
It is only used in A/A.
It is a VPN option.

questions.tip: Preempt is an operational decision: with preempt enabled, after a failover+recovery, the original primary becomes active again (may cause brief interruption); with preempt disabled (recommended), the secondary stays active and the primary returns as passive. Best practice: disable preempt to avoid flapping.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it is not a security feature.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the role and the recommended practice.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it applies to both modes.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is not a VPN feature.

questions.question: What is the main difference between SSL Forward Proxy and SSL Inbound Inspection?

options.text: They are the same, only the name changes.

Forward Proxy decrypts outgoing traffic initiated by internal clients to external servers (server is not trusted). Inbound Inspection decrypts incoming traffic to a known internal server (you control the server).
Inbound decrypts SMTP only.
Forward Proxy does not require a root certificate.

questions.tip: SSL Forward Proxy: internal client initiates connection to internet; firewall does MITM because it does not control the remote server (uses root CA installed on clients). SSL Inbound Inspection: inbound traffic to a server you control; import the server's certificate and create a temporary certificate signed by that CA.

explanations.A: Incorrect: they are functionally different.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the use case for each.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Inbound can decrypt any TLS-based protocol.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Forward Proxy DOES require root CA in clients to reissue certificates.

questions.question: Which protocol is NOT decryptable by Palo Alto without a dedicated external device?

options.text: HTTPS over TLS 1.3
SSH with modern versions and strong encryption
SMTPS
LDAPS

questions.tip: TLS over HTTP/SMTP/LDAP/IMAP/POP3 is decryptable with Forward Proxy or Inbound. SSH is notoriously difficult: keys exchanged via Diffie-Hellman make it hard for the firewall to reissue the certificate easily. For SSH, a dedicated SSH proxy (ProxyCommand or ssh-mitm appliance) is required.

explanations.A: Incorrect: TLS 1.3 is decryptable (with limitations, see q103).
explanations.B: Correct: SSH with strong encryption resists traditional MITM.
explanations.C: Incorrect: SMTPS is TLS and is decryptable.
explanations.D: Incorrect: LDAPS is TLS and is decryptable.

questions.question: With TLS 1.3 enabled on the firewall, what significant change affects the Forward Proxy?

options.text: There are no changes; it works identically.
TLS 1.3 encrypts portions of the handshake, so the Forward Proxy can no longer see the SNI/Server Name Indicator to route to the correct certificate without additional support (ECH, SNI encryption).
TLS 1.3 is incompatible with decryption.
It only works with ECDSA.

questions.tip: TLS 1.3 encrypts portions of the handshake that TLS 1.2 left in the clear (including parts of the certificate). The firewall must rely on fields like SNI (which usually remains in the clear) and apply workarounds. Panorama 10.1+ and PAN-OS 10.1+ introduced better handling of TLS 1.3 with decryption. Always check the support matrix.

explanations.A: Incorrect: There are operational differences.
explanations.B: Correct: Explains the cryptographic challenge of TLS 1.3.
explanations.C: Incorrect: TLS 1.3 can be decrypted with the correct configuration.
explanations.D: Incorrect: TLS 1.3 works with various cipher suites.

questions.question: What is the purpose of installing the firewall's CA root certificate on client machines?

options.text: For the firewall to initiate decryption.
For clients to trust certificates reissued by the firewall during SSL Forward Proxy, avoiding 'your connection is not private' warnings.
To accelerate HTTPS traffic.
To block web pages.

questions.tip: Without the CA root installed, browsers would show certificate errors when the firewall reissues the server's certificate with one signed by its internal CA. It can be distributed via GPO (Active Directory), MDM, or manual installation. Without this, users see warnings and possibly break apps.

explanations.A: Incorrect: The firewall decrypts even if clients do not have the CA; but the warnings break the experience.
explanations.B: Correct: Explains the role of the CA root in the client's chain of trust.
explanations.C: Incorrect: It does not significantly affect performance.
explanations.D: Incorrect: The certificate does not block pages by itself.

questions.question: What is a 'Decryption Exclusion' and when is it used?

options.text: It is a rule to block all encrypted traffic.
It allows excluding specific destinations from decryption (e.g., banking, healthcare) for compliance or technical reasons (certificate pinning, apps that break).
It is synonymous with 'no decrypt' in a single policy.
It is a policy that applies to all users by default.

questions.tip: A 'decryption exclusion' in the decryption profile prevents decryption of certain destinations. Reasons: applications with certificate pinning (which reject MITM and break when reissued), regulatory requirements (PCI-DSS allows controlled exceptions), troubleshooting problematic apps. Document each exclusion with business justification.

explanations.A: Incorrect: An exclusion does not block, it just omits decryption.
explanations.B: Correct: Explains the function and use cases of exclusions.
explanations.C: Incorrect: 'No decrypt' and exclusion are similar but exclusion is more granular.
explanations.D: Incorrect: It applies by destination, not to all by default.

questions.question: Which TCP port does implicit SMTP over TLS (SMTPS) use compared to SMTP with STARTTLS?

options.text: Both use port 25.
SMTPS uses 465 from the start (TLS-in-band). SMTP with STARTTLS starts on 25 (plain) and upgrades to TLS on the same port.
SMTPS uses 587.
STARTTLS uses 465.

questions.tip: SMTPS (465): TLS connection from the first byte. STARTTLS (587 submission or 25): starts in plain text and the client sends 'STARTTLS' to upgrade in-place. The firewall must understand both to decrypt; the SMTP profile in decryption covers STARTTLS.

explanations.A: Incorrect: There is a difference in port and TLS timing.
explanations.B: Correct: Distinguishes the two TLS modes in SMTP.
explanations.C: Incorrect: 587 is for submission (with or without STARTTLS).
explanations.D: Incorrect: STARTTLS operates on the original port (25/587).

questions.question: What happens if you configure SSL Forward Proxy but do NOT install the CA certificate on the clients?

options.text: The firewall does not decrypt anything.
The firewall does decrypt (with its internal CA), but browsers/apps will show severe certificate warnings, breaking the user experience and possibly blocking traffic.
The firewall blocks all HTTPS.
It only works with TLS 1.0.

questions.tip: This is one of the most common mistakes in decryption deployments. The firewall decrypts perfectly, but the user's browser does not trust the reissued certificate (signed by a CA it does not recognize). Result: red screen of 'Your connection is not private'. Fix by distributing the root CA via GPO/MDM solutions.

explanations.A: Incorrect: Decryption still works internally.
explanations.B: Correct: Describes the visible failure for the user.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Only blocks if the policy is configured that way.
explanations.D: Incorrect: TLS 1.0 is deprecated; it is not what limits this.

questions.question: What is the relationship between a Decryption Profile and a Decryption Policy?

options.text: They are the same.
The profile defines WHAT is decrypted (SSL Forward Proxy, SSH, etc.) and CONFIGURATIONS (certificates, protocols). The policy defines TO WHOM it applies (source/destination/zone).
The profile only applies to users.
The policy is optional.

questions.tip: Dual model: Profile + Policy. Profile = type of decryption + CA cert + blocks (e.g., do not decrypt old TLS versions). Policy = matching (zone/source/dest/user) + action (decrypt, no-decrypt). A policy references the profile. Follows the same pattern as Security Profiles + Security Policies.

explanations.A: Incorrect: They have distinct but complementary roles.
explanations.B: Correct: Separates the definition (what) from the matching (whom).
explanations.C: Incorrect: The profile does not discriminate users; the policy can use user as a criterion.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Without a decryption policy, decryption is not applied in a granular way.

questions.question: What does the firewall do when a server uses OCSP Stapling and you have SSL Forward Proxy enabled?

options.text: Blocks the session because it is incompatible.
The firewall handles OCSP correctly: when the server staples the OCSP response, the firewall uses it to validate the server's certificate before reissuing.
Automatically disables OCSP.
It is ignored.

questions.tip: OCSP Stapling improves performance (server signs the OCSP response). The firewall respects the OCSP staple to validate the original certificate before reissuing a new one signed by the internal CA. If the certificate is revoked, the firewall blocks or logs according to configuration.

explanations.A: Incorrect: OCSP Stapling is compatible.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the interaction with OCSP Stapling.
explanations.C: Incorrect: the firewall does not disable the server's OCSP.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it actively uses it for validation.

questions.question: What is 'URL Filtering Re-Enable' in a Decryption Profile and what is it for?

options.text: Disables URL Filtering on HTTPS sites.
Allows the firewall to apply URL Filtering on the actual content (SNI, HTTP headers) after decryption, not just on the resolved DNS name (which could be a CDN).
Only works with HTTP/1.1.
It is an obsolete option.

questions.tip: Without decryption, URL/URL-category is based on the DNS hostname/SNI. But many services (Cloudfront, Akamai) resolve the same IP for thousands of domains. Once decrypted, the firewall sees the true URL in the HTTP header and can categorize it correctly.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it does the opposite, it re-enables URL filtering post-decryption.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the value of decrypting for accurate URL classification.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it works with HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is still a recommended option in modern profiles.

questions.question: What does the 'decrypt-failure' log show and how is it used for troubleshooting?

options.text: Only fatal errors that stop traffic.
Records sessions that the firewall attempted to decrypt but failed (invalid certificate, unsupported TLS version, handshake error). It is used to identify problematic sites or exclusion candidates.
Only client-side errors.
Only fills up when there are hardware errors.

questions.tip: The decrypt-failure log is invaluable for adjusting exclusions and detecting common problems. Typical errors: 'unsupported protocol' (old TLS), 'cert expired' (site not renewing), 'cert untrusted' (untrusted CA). Monitoring the volume of errors is key to healthy operation.

explanations.A: Incorrect: also logs non-fatal failures (sessions allowed despite failure).
explanations.B: Correct: describes the utility of the log for troubleshooting.
explanations.C: Incorrect: captures firewall errors, not just client-side errors.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is a software log, not a hardware log.

questions.question: What is a primary operational benefit of decrypting corporate HTTPS traffic?

options.text: Saves bandwidth.
Allows App-ID, Threat Prevention, URL Filtering, DLP, and other profiles to see the actual content and apply granular controls, not just IP/port blocking.
Accelerates the web connection.
Reduces the firewall's CPU usage.

questions.tip: Without decryption, the firewall sees IPs and ports but not the content. With decryption, it sees detailed App-ID (slack vs web-browsing), exact URLs for filtering, payloads for DLP, and threat signatures. It is the fundamental enabler for effective security on encrypted traffic.

explanations.A: Incorrect: decryption consumes CPU; it does not save bandwidth.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the business value of decryption.
explanations.C: Incorrect: decryption can add latency.
explanations.D: Incorrect: CPU usage increases, not decreases.

questions.question: What does WildFire do with a suspicious file it receives?

options.text: Deletes it immediately.
Sends it to Palo Alto's cloud for execution in an isolated sandbox, static + dynamic analysis, and shares verdicts with WildFire's global network.
Only scans it with antivirus.
Encrypts and archives it.

questions.tip: WildFire is a sandbox-based malware analysis service. It receives suspicious files (PE, Office, PDF, APK, etc.), detonates them in virtual machines (Windows, Linux, macOS, Android), observes behavior (network, registry, persistence), and generates signatures that are distributed to the entire WildFire network.

explanations.A: Incorrect: the original file is not deleted; it is executed in the sandbox.
explanations.B: Correct: explains WildFire's workflow.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is much more than AV; it is dynamic detonation.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it does not encrypt; it analyzes and discards.

questions.question: How is a file sent to WildFire when detected via firewall?

options.text: The firewall automatically uploads all files.
The firewall has a WildFire Analysis Profile that indicates which extensions/sizes to send. The suspicious file is uploaded to the cloud (wildfire.paloaltonetworks.com) for detonation.
Only via email.
Requires a VPN to Palo Alto.

questions.tip: WildFire Analysis Profile defines: extensions (pe, exe, dll, pdf, doc, docx, xlsx, apk, etc.), max file size (typically 10MB), and whether to forward 'grayware' or 'phishing'. The firewall sends hash and content, receives verdict in seconds to minutes. Available as public or private WildFire (WF-500 appliance).

explanations.A: Incorrect: only sends according to the configured profile.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the sending mechanism and profile.
explanations.C: Incorrect: can send any file from any traffic.
explanations.D: Incorrect: uses public internet, not VPN.

questions.question: What types of verdicts can WildFire return?

options.text: Only 'malware' or 'clean'.
Benign, Malware, Grayware, Phishing. Additionally 'Pending' while analyzing.
Only 'suspicious' or 'safe'.
Numeric from 0 to 10.

questions.tip: WildFire Verdicts: Benign (clean), Malware (confirmed malicious), Grayware (not malware but unwanted behavior: adware, PUP), Phishing (attempted credential theft). The verdict is translated into a signature and distributed globally. Pending = still analyzing.

explanations.A: Incorrect: there are more possible states.
explanations.B: Correct: lists the standard verdicts.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it does not use that terminology.
explanations.D: Incorrect: there is no numeric scoring in WildFire.

questions.question: What distinguishes WildFire Public Cloud from a WildFire Private Cloud (WF-500)?

options.text: They are identical.
Public Cloud: shares global intelligence with all users; files go to Palo Alto's cloud. WF-500 (Private): the appliance stays local, files do not leave the organization, ideal for regulated sectors.
WF-500 is only log storage.
Public Cloud requires dedicated hardware.

questions.tip: WF-500 (2U appliance): runs WildFire 100% locally, samples and verdicts stay within the datacenter. Complies with data sovereignty requirements (government, defense, banking). Public Cloud is the default option: scalable, aggregated global intelligence, but samples go to Palo Alto.

explanations.A: Incorrect: they have clear differences.
explanations.B: Correct: distinguishes the two deployment models.
explanations.C: Incorrect: WF-500 runs the real sandbox, not just logs.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Public Cloud is SaaS, no client hardware required.

questions.question: How is WildFire integrated with email (smtp) on the firewall?

options.text: It is not integrated.
WildFire Analysis Profile applied to incoming (or outgoing) SMTP traffic uploads suspicious attachments. Complemented with Anti-Spyware + Vulnerability Protection + Antivirus.
It only blocks known extensions.
It requires an Office add-on.

questions.tip: When an email arrives with a PE/doc/PDF attachment, the firewall extracts the file, compares it with local signatures (AV), and if it passes to dynamic analysis, it sends it to WildFire. The Antivirus + Anti-Spyware + Vulnerability Protection + WildFire Analysis profiles operate in layers. The idea is zero-day protection: AV local signature, WildFire zero-day.

explanations.A: Incorrect: integration is central to the email protection flow.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the defense-in-depth architecture.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it detects zero-days, not just extensions.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it operates at the network level, no add-in required.

questions.question: What is 'WildFire Forwarding' in the context of a firewall cluster?

options.text: Forward WildFire logs.
Allows multiple firewalls (including Panorama and other branches) to forward samples to the same WildFire for correlation and consistent verdict.
A firewall policy.
It is the migration to Public Cloud.

questions.tip: WildFire Forwarding ensures that if a branch detects a sample, it is forwarded to the configured WildFire (which can be the centralized WF-500). This way, a single verdict serves all firewalls in the cluster. Configurable via Panorama to manage large clusters.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it is not just log forwarding.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the consolidation of samples in distributed topologies.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not a security policy.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is not cloud migration.

questions.question: How long does WildFire typically take to deliver a verdict?

options.text: Several days.
From seconds to minutes: benign files in <5s, evasive malware up to several minutes. For very complex files, it may take longer, but initial verdicts are usually fast.
Always exactly 1 hour.
Only in real-time during download.

questions.tip: WildFire runs multiple engines (static, dynamic, machine learning) in parallel. Clean files are identified immediately. Sophisticated malware may require several detonation iterations (e.g., sandbox-evasion delays). Under normal conditions, AV signatures are updated in 5-15 minutes.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it would be useless for operational protection.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the realistic time ranges.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not a fixed SLA.
explanations.D: Incorrect: WildFire processes offline, not inline in real-time.

questions.question: What does 'WildFire Signature' mean and how is it distributed?

options.text: It is only a hash of the file.
A signature is a behavior pattern or hash that WildFire generates after confirming a malware verdict. It is shared globally via ThreatVault/Antivirus/Anti-Spyware updates, every 5-15 minutes.
It is a PDF document.
It is only shared if you pay extra.

questions.tip: WildFire generates signatures that are injected into Antivirus, Anti-Spyware, and DNS signatures. Automatic distribution (every 5-15 minutes during active hours) to all firewalls subscribed to the service. If a firewall in Japan detects malware, minutes later all firewalls worldwide have the signature.

explanations.A: Incorrect: includes hash + patterns + behavior.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the distribution mechanism.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not a document.
explanations.D: Incorrect: distribution is part of the base service.

questions.question: What is 'WildFire API' and what are its use cases?

options.text: A network interface between firewalls.
A REST API that allows external applications (SOAR, SIEM, EDR) to send files for programmatic analysis and receive verdicts. Ideal for integration with private sandboxes.
A secret CLI console.
Only for partners.

questions.tip: WildFire API (https://wildfire.paloaltonetworks.com/publicapi): publicapi/get-verdict (verdict by hash), publicapi/submit/file (submit sample), publicapi/submit/url (submit URL). Used by SIEMs (Splunk, Elasticsearch) and threat intel tools to enrich alerts with WildFire verdicts.

explanations.A: Incorrect: It is not a network protocol.
explanations.B: Correct: It describes the purpose and use cases of the API.
explanations.C: Incorrect: It is not a CLI.
explanations.D: Incorrect: It is open to service customers.

questions.question: What advantages does 'WildFire Inline ML' (local machine learning) have over WildFire cloud?

options.text: It is less accurate.
Inline ML runs local ML models on the firewall, providing sub-second verdicts without sending the sample to the cloud, ideal for limited bandwidth or regulatory constraints.
It requires special hardware.
It only works with PE files.

questions.tip: WildFire Inline ML (introduced in PAN-OS 10.0): optimized models that run in the firewall's datapath, evaluates PE files in milliseconds and blocks known malware before WildFire completes analysis. Complements (does not replace) WildFire cloud for evasive cases.

explanations.A: Incorrect: It is very accurate for its scope.
explanations.B: Correct: It explains the value of having local + cloud ML.
explanations.C: Incorrect: It runs on existing PA-Series / VM-Series.
explanations.D: Incorrect: It covers multiple types, not just PE.

questions.question: How to configure WildFire to NOT send certain files for privacy reasons?

options.text: WildFire does not allow exclusion.

In the WildFire Analysis Profile, 'File Forwarding' can be configured selectively or use WildFire Private (WF-500) to keep everything local. Files can also be excluded by extension or use the 'do not forward' option in specific profiles.
Only via a disconnected physical firewall.
A special license is required.

questions.tip: While WildFire Public Cloud encrypts and protects samples, organizations with strict regulations may prefer WF-500 (all local). An intermediate alternative: WildFire Analysis Profile can exclude file types (e.g., DLP-sensitive extensions) and only upload samples that do not contain that data.

explanations.A: Incorrect: There are control options.
explanations.B: Correct: It explains the available options.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Air-gap is not required.
explanations.D: Incorrect: WF-500 requires a license but the exclusion profile is standard.

questions.question: What is the 'WildFire Cluster' and what is it used for?

options.text: It is a load balancer.
It allows consolidating multiple WF-500 appliances into a cluster for high availability and scalability, distributing the analysis load across nodes.
It is synonymous with WildFire API.
It is just marketing.

questions.tip: WildFire Cluster groups up to 20 WF-500 nodes into a scalable HA cluster. When an organization processes a high volume of suspicious files, a single WF-500 can become saturated. The cluster distributes the workload, offers failover, and increases throughput.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it is not L4 load balancing.
explanations.B: Correct: it explains the purpose of the cluster.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is a feature of the appliance, not of the API.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is a real documented capability.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the CLI command 'test security-policy-match' in Palo Alto?

options.text: Only to view configuration.
It allows simulating which security rule would apply to a given traffic: source/dest/zone/user/app/service/url. Useful for understanding why a rule does not match.
It restarts the firewall.
It only serves for HA.

questions.tip: 'test security-policy-match from <zone> to <zone> source <ip> destination <ip> protocol <proto> destination-port <port>' shows which rule matched (or none). It is essential for understanding why a specific traffic is being allowed/denied. Similar to 'show running security-policy' + simulation.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it performs a dynamic simulation.
explanations.B: Correct: it describes the use and purpose of the command.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it does not restart anything.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it applies to security policies, not to HA.

questions.question: Where can the detailed flow of a session (including routing, NAT, App-ID decisions) be viewed in the GUI?

options.text: Main Dashboard.
Monitor > Logs > Traffic. Each entry shows source, destination, app, rule, NAT (post-NAT IPs/ports), bytes, time. ACC (Application Command Center) can also be used for aggregated views.
In the configuration PDF.
Only in CLI.

questions.tip: Traffic logs (Monitor > Logs > Traffic) are the first place to see what is happening with a session. Key fields: Application (important: App-ID), Rule, Source Address (pre-NAT), Destination Address (post-NAT if there is NAT), bytes/packets, session end reason.

explanations.A: Incorrect: the dashboard shows aggregated stats, not sessions.
explanations.B: Correct: it identifies where the per-session details are.
explanations.C: Incorrect: the PDF does not have dynamic logs.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is also in CLI (show log traffic), but the GUI is more user-friendly.

questions.question: Which CLI command shows the current management connections to the firewall and from where?

options.text: show running mgmt
show management-clients: lists active connections to the management interface, useful for detecting unauthorized access or hung connections.
show users all
show netstat

questions.tip: The 'show management-clients' command is useful in operations: shows SSH/HTTPS connections to the MGT interface, the associated user, idle time, and from which IP. If you see suspicious connections, you should invalidate them (e.g., 'delete management-clients <session-id>') or log them out via 'request logout user'.

explanations.A: Incorrect: show running mgmt does not exist.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the command and its use.
explanations.C: Incorrect: show users all lists administrative users, not connected clients.
explanations.D: Incorrect: netstat is not available in Palo Alto's CLI.

questions.question: Which log do you check first if you suspect the Data Plane is saturated?

options.text: The purchase PDF.
Monitor > System. Look for 'data-plane-cpu' or 'session-utilization' near 100%. For maximum sessions use 'show session info' in CLI.
Only Threat logs.
GlobalProtect logs.

questions.tip: Data plane saturation manifests in: active session drops, high packet latency, ping packet loss even with good management CPU. Check: session utilization (max sessions vs used), packet buffer drops, and data-plane CPU. Also interface counters for errors (CRC, drops).

explanations.A: Incorrect: it is not an operational log.
explanations.B: Correct: describes where and what to look for.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Threat logs do not show saturation.
explanations.D: Incorrect: GlobalProtect logs are for VPN clients.

questions.question: How do you capture packets directly on a firewall interface for analysis (e.g., Wireshark)?

options.text: It is impossible on Palo Alto.
Use 'tcpdump' in CLI: 'tcpdump filter "src host X and port Y" <interface>'. The .pcap files are stored on the firewall and can be downloaded via SCP.
Only via GUI with a secret plugin.
Requires purchasing Wireshark Enterprise.

questions.tip: Palo Alto exposes tcpdump in its CLI (based on FreeBSD packet filter). Useful options: -i <interface>, -w <file>, -s <snaplen>, -c <count>. For advanced troubleshooting, it is recommended to use Packet Capture (Monitor > Packet Capture) in the GUI with filters, supports stage 1-4 (firewall stage allows viewing before/after processing).

explanations.A: Incorrect: tcpdump is available.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the usage and the GUI alternative.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Packet Capture is in the native GUI, not a plugin.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Wireshark is free, does not require Enterprise.

questions.question: What does a session with 'session end reason: tcp-rst-from-server' indicate?

options.text: The client aborted.
The server sent a TCP RST (reset), indicating it closed the connection abruptly. Typical of: application crash, server overloaded, intermediate firewall blocking.
It is a success message.
The firewall intentionally closed the session.

questions.tip: TCP RST is a signal for abrupt closure (vs FIN which is orderly). Common causes: server app crash, intermediate WAF/proxy blocking, syn-flood/replay detected. Investigate with packet capture on the server side to confirm.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it would be 'tcp-fin-from-client' or similar.
explanations.B: Correct: explains what RST is and why it occurs.
explanations.C: Incorrect: RST is a signal for error/abort, not success.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it would be 'tcp-fin' from the firewall or 'denied'.

questions.question: Which command shows the state of the high availability (HA) cluster?

options.text: show running ha
show high-availability state + show ha active-passive (or show ha active-active for A/A). Shows local role, peer state, sync status.
ping peer
show ip route

questions.tip: Key HA commands: 'show high-availability state' (global state), 'show ha active-passive' or 'show ha active-active' (details of A/P or A/A), 'show ha link-monitoring' and 'show ha path-monitoring' (state of heartbeats and path monitoring), 'show session sync' (state of session sync).

explanations.A: Incorrect: show running does not show runtime state.
explanations.B: Correct: lists the key HA commands.
explanations.C: Incorrect: ping is basic connectivity.
explanations.D: Incorrect: routes do not show HA.

questions.question: If HA is not synchronizing configuration between peers, what do you check first?

options.text: The system clock.
That both peers have the same 'HA Configuration Sync' enabled and that the HA1/HA2 interface is connected and operational. Use 'show ha state' on both peers.
The Java version.
The antivirus.

questions.tip: Troubleshooting steps for sync issues: 1) Verify HA1 cabling (sync config) and HA2 (session sync). 2) Confirm identical config (same interfaces, IP, encryption keys). 3) Check HA daemon logs (ha-agent.log). 4) Validate identical PAN-OS versions. 5) If persistence不一致, force 'request high-availability sync-to-remote now'.

explanations.A: Incorrect: the clock affects logs but not config sync.
explanations.B: Correct: lists the troubleshooting steps.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Palo Alto does not use Java.
explanations.D: Incorrect: the antivirus does not impact HA.

questions.question: What is 'session owner' in HA active-active and why is it important?

options.text: It is the firewall with the highest CPU.

In A/A, each firewall is the 'owner' of certain sessions (based on the hash of the session tuple). The peer acts as a 'backup' and takes over the session if the owner fails. This implies: floating IP is not required for A/A, packets can arrive at any firewall but are redirected to the owner.
It is the historical primary firewall.
It is synonymous with active device.

questions.tip: A/A uses the session owner concept: the first packet of a session determines the owner (hash of 5-tuple). The peer synchronizes the session as a backup. If the owner fails, the backup forwards the traffic to maintain the session. This enables A/A without the need for traditional L3 failover.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it is not based on CPU but on the hash of the tuple.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the concept of owner/backup in A/A.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not a historical concept.
explanations.D: Incorrect: active device is for A/P; owner is for A/A.

questions.question: What is a typical cause of 'packet buffer drops' in a Palo Alto firewall and how is it diagnosed?

options.text: Long network cable.

Microbursts (short bursts that exceed contracted bandwidth but last briefly), high concurrency of new sessions, or jumbo packets. Use 'show running resource-monitor' and 'show counter global | match drop'.
Ambient temperature.
Only occurs in new hardware.

questions.tip: Packet buffer drops indicate that the firewall cannot process traffic at the rate it is arriving. In a modern profile, review: show counter global delta (look for drop counters), show running resource-monitor (CPU/memory usage), and show interface <x> (errors and utilization). Causes include: microbursts, DDoS, misconfigured jumbo frames, irregular IMIX usage.

explanations.A: Incorrect: the cable is not the cause.
explanations.B: Correct: lists common causes and diagnostic tools.
explanations.C: Incorrect: thermal does not affect packet buffer logic.
explanations.D: Incorrect: occurs in any hardware under stress.

questions.question: Where do you look for evidence of a reconnaissance attack (port scan) on the firewall?

options.text: Only in the browser.
Monitor > Logs > Threat. Scan attempts generate Vulnerability Protection signatures (e.g., 'TCP port scan'). Also in ACC under 'Threat Activity' and 'Scan Activity'.
In the manufacturer's PDF.
Only in WildFire logs.

questions.tip: Vulnerability Protection detects scan signatures: TCP SYN scan, port sweep, etc. Configurable to 'block' or 'alert'. If you see many 'low trust' IPs scanning, consider adding them to the blocked IPs list in EDL (External Dynamic List) or applying a deny rule at the beginning of the policy.

explanations.A: Incorrect: not in the browser.
explanations.B: Correct: identifies where the threat logs are.
explanations.C: Incorrect: docs are not operational.
explanations.D: Incorrect: scan is Vulnerability Protection, not WildFire.

questions.question: If a customer reports that a certain HTTPS site is not loading and suspects a decryption issue, what is the first step?

options.text: Format the firewall.
Check 'decrypt-failure' logs filtering by destination IP or hostname. Confirm if the site has certificate pinning, exotic TLS version, or is in a known exclusion.
Change the root CA.
Increase MTU across the segment.

questions.tip: Sites that break with decryption often have: certificate pinning (e.g., mobile apps, banking APIs), TLS 1.3 + ECH, sessions with old tickets. Diagnostic tools: decrypt-failure log + packet capture (mirror port or inline) + tests with curl --ssl-no-revoke. Document each necessary exclusion.

explanations.A: Incorrect: waste of time and configuration.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the correct diagnostic approach.
explanations.C: Incorrect: changing the root CA without an identified cause does not resolve anything.
explanations.D: Incorrect: MTU is not relevant to decryption.

questions.question: How do you identify if the Management CPU is high on the firewall?

options.text: Looking at the equipment lights.
Dashboard > System Resources or 'show running resource-monitor' in CLI. If mgmt-CPU > 50% sustained, investigate logs (commit, config, reports) or excessive management packets.
Only via remote SNMP.
It is impossible to see.

questions.tip: High mgmt CPU is often due to: ongoing commit operations, report/tech support generation, log forwarding, SNMP polling, or DDoS attacks on the management port. If sustained, consider blocking ping/access to the MGT interface or moving it to a private network.

explanations.A: Incorrect: lights do not show CPU.
explanations.B: Correct: describes how to identify and possible causes.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it can also be seen internally without SNMP.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is completely visible.

questions.question: Which CLI command shows the BGP status on the firewall?

options.text: show bgp stats
show routing protocol bgp peer + show routing protocol bgp route. Also 'show ip bgp summary'.
ping bgp
show ospf bgp

questions.tip: BGP debugging in Palo: 'show routing protocol bgp peer' (state of neighbors, established/active/idle), 'show routing protocol bgp route' (BGP table received), 'show routing protocol bgp adv' (advertised routes), 'request routing protocol bgp restart'. For specific events: 'tail mp-log ms.log' or filtered logs.

explanations.A: Incorrect: there is no show bgp stats.
explanations.B: Correct: lists the key BGP commands.
explanations.C: Incorrect: ping is for connectivity, not BGP state.
explanations.D: Incorrect: there is no such command.

questions.question: What is the 'tech support file' and when should you generate it?

options.text: It is a user manual.

It is a file with logs, configuration, and counters from the firewall, generated via 'request tech-support dump' or from the GUI (Device > Tech Support). It is sent to TAC for advanced troubleshooting.
It is only for licenses.
It is a speed test.

questions.tip: Tech support file includes: running config, log dumps (config, system, traffic), system info, counters, and lots of operational information. It does NOT include passwords or private keys. Generate when TAC requests it; it may take several minutes. For sensitive data, verify with your security team before sharing.

explanations.A: Incorrect: it is not a manual.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the content and purpose.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not for licenses.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it does not measure speed.

questions.question: What does 'request restart service' do on the Palo Alto firewall?

options.text: Reboots the entire appliance.
Restarts a specific service (e.g., iked, mgmtsrv, dnsproxy, routed, snmpd, sslmgr). Useful for diagnosing stuck services without affecting traffic forwarding.
Clears logs.
Changes the password.

questions.tip: The command 'request restart service <service>' allows restarting individual services. Common services: iked (IKE VPN), sslmgr (SSL/TLS mgmt), mgmtsrv (web/CLI management), routed (routing daemon). It does NOT interrupt the data plane. Use when a service is hung but we don't want to affect traffic.

explanations.A: Incorrect: to reboot everything, use 'request reboot'.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the command and typical services.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it does not clear logs.
explanations.D: Incorrect: use a different command for that.

questions.question: Which type of log do you review to investigate user authentication failures (e.g., GlobalProtect, Captive Portal)?

options.text: Traffic.
Authentication logs (Monitor > Logs > Authentication). Shows successful/failed attempts, source IP, user, method (LDAP/RADIUS/local), and error message.
WildFire logs.
Only via CLI debug.

questions.tip: Authentication logs are separate and specific. Key fields: user, auth method, server profile, success/failure, error message. Common errors: 'user not found' (typo or AD sync), 'invalid password' (credentials), 'auth server timeout' (connectivity to LDAP/RADIUS), 'disabled account'.

explanations.A: Incorrect: traffic does not include auth fail/success.
explanations.B: Correct: identifies the correct log type.
explanations.C: Incorrect: WildFire is for malware, not auth.
explanations.D: Incorrect: the log is also in the GUI.

questions.question: If a Virtual Wire (V-Wire) is not forwarding traffic, what should you check first?

options.text: The temperature of the equipment.

That the physical ports assigned to the V-Wire are correct (e.g., ethernet1/3 + ethernet1/4), that they are administratively up, and that the interfaces are without incorrect tags. Use 'show interface <x>'.
The BGP configuration.
Only the loopback.

questions.tip: V-Wire is 'bump in the wire': the firewall forwards traffic at layer 2 between two designated interfaces. If it is not forwarding: 1) Verify the physical link (lights, show interface hardware-status). 2) Confirm the port and tag (subinterface). 3) Validate that the cable is in the correct port. 4) Check ARP on interfaces.

explanations.A: Incorrect: temperature is not the cause.
explanations.B: Correct: lists the key checks.
explanations.C: Incorrect: V-Wire does not use BGP.
explanations.D: Incorrect: V-Wire does not use loopback.

questions.question: How do you verify that an IPSec tunnel establishes IKE Phase 1 correctly?

options.text: By looking at the peer's browser.
Use 'show vpn ike-sa': shows the IKE Security Association, with source/destination IP, status, negotiated auth/encryption, remaining lifetimes.
Only with external Wireshark.
It is impossible to verify.

questions.tip: If the IKE SA does not appear or is 'down', check: pre-shared keys or certificates, correct source/destination IPs, compatible IKE version, matching proposals. Log: System > Logs > System with subtype 'ike-negotiation'. For Phase 2 (IPSec SA): 'show vpn ipsec-sa' or 'show vpn ipsec-sa tunnel <name>'.

explanations.A: Incorrect: not in the browser.
explanations.B: Correct: identifies the command and how to interpret it.
explanations.C: Incorrect: there is a native command.
explanations.D: Incorrect: it is visible and operational.

questions.question: What is a 'fault' in the context of Palo Alto and how is it raised?

options.text: A misconfigured policy.
A persistent failure state (e.g., physical port down, expired subscription, panic disk). Visible in the Dashboard as 'System Alerts' and in CLI 'show system state'.
A typing error by the user.
A successful test.

questions.tip: Faults are system or hardware level issues that require attention. Types: link-down (physical interface), sw-version-mismatch, cert-expired, panic, degraded, fan-failed. They can be viewed via Dashboard, 'request ack fault <id>' (acknowledge), or 'clear fault <id>' if already resolved.

explanations.A: Incorrect: policies do not generate system-level faults.
explanations.B: Correct: explains what a fault is and how to view it.
explanations.C: Incorrect: it is not a user error.
explanations.D: Incorrect: successful test generates info, not a fault.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence to perform a 'pcap' (packet capture) using the GUI in Palo Alto?

options.text: Restart the firewall first.

Monitor > Packet Capture > Add. Configure: Stage (1=raw pre-policy, 2=post-NAT, 3=post-decrypt, 4=post-policy), interface, filter (IP/port/proto), file size. Start capture, then Stop and Download .pcap.
Only via CLI with tcpdump.
Requires premium license.

questions.tip: Packet Capture (GUI) is powerful because it allows you to see packets in 4 stages. Stage 1 sees the raw packet before the firewall. Stage 2 sees after NAT (ip nat-post). Stage 3 sees after decrypt. Stage 4 sees after policy (drop, allow). This is invaluable for understanding why something was decided.

explanations.A: Incorrect: Restarting is not necessary.
explanations.B: Correct: Lists the steps and key options.
explanations.C: Incorrect: GUI is preferable for visualizing stages.
explanations.D: Incorrect: Packet Capture comes with the platform.

questions.question: Which CLI command helps you understand why a security policy does not match for particular traffic?

options.text: show firewall rules
test security-policy-match from <zone> to <zone> source <ip> destination <ip> destination-port <port> protocol <tcp> application <app>
list security
trace policy

questions.tip: The 'test security-policy-match' command is the first step for policy troubleshooting. Optional but useful parameters: user, application, service, category, url. Output: name of the rule that matched + the prefix (1-7) or 'no rule matched' (deny implicit). For decryption policies: 'test decryption-policy-match'.

explanations.A: Incorrect: show firewall rules does not simulate.
explanations.B: Correct: Describes the command and its parameters.
explanations.C: Incorrect: list is for enumerating, not simulating.
explanations.D: Incorrect: trace policy does not exist.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'show session all' and 'show session filter source <IP>'?

options.text: They are equivalent.
'show session all' lists ALL active sessions (could be thousands on a busy firewall). 'show session filter source <IP>' filters only those of a specific IP, useful for focused troubleshooting.
'show session all' also shows closed sessions.
'show session filter' does not exist in PAN-OS.

questions.tip: 'show session all' is useful for global statistics but overwhelming on large firewalls. 'show session filter <criteria>' allows filtering by source/destination/application/etc. Combine filters to investigate: 'show session filter source 10.1.1.50 destination 200.50.50.50'.

explanations.A: Incorrect: They are clearly different.
explanations.B: Correct: Describes the use case for each.
explanations.C: Incorrect: Only shows active sessions.
explanations.D: Incorrect: filter exists and is key.

questions.question: A session shows 'application: insufficient-data'. What does it mean?

options.text: It is a fatal error.
Insufficient-data = App-ID has not received enough information from the flow to identify the app (e.g., too few packets before timeout). Typically very short connections or non-standard traffic.
It is always malware.
It is the default app.

questions.tip: When App-ID does not have enough information to classify, it assigns 'insufficient-data'. Causes: very short connection (syn-flood, scan), non-standard traffic, fragmented packets. Investigate with packet capture to understand what traffic is passing.

explanations.A: Incorrect: indicates incomplete classification, not a fatal error.
explanations.B: Correct: explains the context.
explanations.C: Incorrect: does not imply malware.
explanations.D: Incorrect: 'unknown' or 'incomplete' are different.

questions.question: To diagnose a routing problem on the firewall, what CLI command is most useful?

options.text: show system info
show routing route + test routing <source> <destination> to simulate and see what route would be taken.
show arp
show jobs all

questions.tip: 'show routing route' shows the RIB (all known routes). For active diagnostics, use 'test routing <ip-source> <ip-destination>' which simulates the route lookup the firewall would do for real traffic, including routing policy and PBF.

explanations.A: Incorrect: system info is general metadata.
explanations.B: Correct: describes the routing diagnostic flow.
explanations.C: Incorrect: ARP is layer 2, not routing.
explanations.D: Incorrect: jobs are async tasks.

questions.question: How does a site-to-site VPN identify that the tunnel is actually active (vs. just configured)?

options.text: The fact of having config does not guarantee an active tunnel; use 'show vpn ike-sa' (IKE Security Associations) and 'show vpn ipsec-sa' to see active SAs. If there are SAs, the tunnel is UP.
Look at the green light on the firewall.
Wait 24 hours.
Just by reading the config.

questions.tip: The config may be present but the IKE SA / IPSec SA are what indicate active tunnels. Without active SAs, the tunnel is down even if it is configured. Also use 'show vpn ike-sa gateway <gw-name>' for detail.

explanations.A: Correct: SAs are the real proof.
explanations.B: Incorrect: lights do not confirm tunnels.
explanations.C: Incorrect: there is immediate diagnosis.
explanations.D: Incorrect: config without SAs = tunnel down.
